title: Interface Piezoelectric Loss in Superconducting Qubits
authors: Haoxin Zhou, Kangdi Yu, Yashwanth Balaji, Sanjit Shirol, Leo Sementilli, Zi-Huai Zhang, Adam Schwartzberg, Alp Sipahigil
abstract: Dissipation remains a central obstacle to improving superconducting quantum circuits, yet the microscopic origins of loss in widely used materials platforms are not fully understood. Here, we report the observation of interface piezoelectricity-induced dissipation in superconducting qubits fabricated on high-resistivity silicon. Our devices use a transmon qubit with a shunt capacitor that simultaneously serves as an interdigital transducer embedded in a surface acoustic wave resonator. By tuning the qubit transition into resonance with discrete mechanical modes, we observe up to a factor-of-two reduction in qubit lifetime, consistent with energy exchange between the qubit and mechanical modes mediated by piezoelectric coupling at the aluminum-silicon interface. Our findings provide direct evidence for interface piezoelectricity as a distinct loss channel in superconducting qubits. Combined with multiphysics simulations, these findings suggest that interface piezoelectric loss can dominate over loss from two-level systems at sufficiently high frequencies.
summary: This paper investigates interface piezoelectricity as a dominant decoherence source in superconducting qubits fabricated on silicon. By coupling the qubit to SAW resonators, the authors experimentally and numerically demonstrate that energy loss into acoustic phonons significantly limits qubit lifetime. The findings are crucial for advancing quantum computing by identifying a structural loss channel and proposing device-level engineering solutions to mitigate it.
main_problem: Dissipation, particularly interface piezoelectric loss, remains a major obstacle limiting the coherence time of superconducting quantum qubits.
main_result: The study provides direct evidence that interface piezoelectricity acts as a distinct, measurable loss channel, which can dominate over TLS loss at sufficiently high frequencies.
method: The research combines experimental measurements of qubit decay rates with detailed multiphysics simulations (using finite-element methods) to model the energy exchange between the qubit and acoustic phonons.
model_or_system: The system involves a transmon qubit coupled to a Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) resonator on a silicon substrate. The physics is modeled using coupled Hamiltonian/Langevin equations describing the interaction between the superconducting circuit and the mechanical acoustic modes.
key_observables: Qubit lifetime ($T_1$), excited-state population ($p_e$), and the frequency dependence of the loss rate ($\Gamma_{	ext{piezo}} \propto f^{3.4}$).
important_parameters: Interface Energy Participation Ratio (EPR), coupling strength ($g_{m,k}$), and the crossover frequency where piezoelectric loss dominates (predicted near 10 GHz).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on distinguishing frequency-independent mechanical resonances from bias-voltage-shifting TLS features, and modeling the coupling via effective admittance calculations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate device structures, measurement protocols (e.g., $p_e$ vs. $V_{bias}$), and simulation results showing the frequency scaling of the predicted piezoelectric decay rate compared to other loss sources.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the general problem of dissipation to proposing the piezoelectric mechanism, detailing the experimental diagnostic techniques (using SAW resonators and bias sweeping), and finally presenting theoretical models and simulations to quantify the loss and suggest structural mitigation strategies.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it tackles a fundamental decoherence mechanism in superconducting qubits, providing quantitative scaling laws ($\Gamma \propto f^{3.4}$) and engineering guidelines (metamaterials) for improving quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Detecting interface piezoelectric qubit-phonon coupling. a, Pulse sequence used to probe the effect of electromechanical cou- pling at a fixed delay time tdelay = τ0 = 3 µs following qubit exci- tation. b, Simulated electric-field distribution generated by applying Vbias = 1 V to the XY line. Scale bar represents 30 µm. c, The stan- dard tunneling model of a TLS. An applied voltage bias Vbias shifts the TLS transition energy ℏωTLS. d, Qubit excited state population pe measured at delay time τ0 as a function of qubit frequency fq and TLS-tuning bias voltage Vbias. e, Bias-averaged pe at fixed delay time τ0, revealing the spectral signatures of the SAW resonator modes. f, Reference...

FIG. 3. Qubit lifetime reduction due to piezoelectric coupling to SAW resonator modes. a, Pulse sequence used for coherence mea- surements. b, Example of an energy-relaxation (T1) characterization measured at fq =3.14 GHz. c, Example of a Ramsey (T ∗ 2 ) character- ization measured at fq =3.14 GHz. d, Bias-averaged T ∗ 2 as a func- tion of qubit frequency. The decreasing trend of T ∗ 2 away from the sweet spot is captured by the flux sensitivity T ∗ 2 ∝(∂fq/∂Iflux)−1

FIG. 4. Predicted frequency dependence of interface piezoelec- tric loss for superconducting qubits. All quantities are obtained from finite-element simulations of an aluminum-on-silicon coplanar capacitor. Schematic a-b, Top (a) and side (b) views of the simu- lated capacitor. Axial symmetry is applied to reduce computational complexity. Features are not drawn to scale. c, Circuit model of the superconducting qubit studied. Y11(ω) is the effective admittance of the capacitor, as seen by the Josephson junction LJ, including piezo- electric coupling in a multiphysics simulation. d, Simulated EPR- dependent quality factor of the qubit at fq = ωq/2π = 4.5 GHz.
